summary: I attended grammar and junior high school near Hiroshima, and there was a girls' high school in Hiroshima City that I wished I could have gone to. During my school years, we took excursions to places like Kure and Itsukushima, which are near Hiroshima. My husband was originally from Hiroshima, and he had been in America before returning to Japan to marry me. Hiroshima was the backdrop of much of my early life and education before I came to America in 1917.
cues: girls' high school in Hiroshima City

summary: I remember going on school excursions to Kure, which is a naval port near where I lived. We walked there during our junior high school days, and it was a significant trip for us since we usually walked everywhere and couldn't go too far. Visiting Kure was part of my school experiences along with Itsukushima, and those trips left a strong impression on me even though my feet hurt from the long walks.

One of the most memorable school excursions I took was to Itsukushima, which is known as one of the three most picturesque places in Japan. We walked there from school, and I remember my feet hurting from the long journey. It was a special trip during my junior high school days, and it remains vivid in my memory as a beautiful place I visited with my classmates.
cues: one of the three most picturesque places in Japan

summary: The Russo-Japanese War broke out the same year my husband took his physical examination for the draft. Because he had a crippled hand from an accident, he was exempted from military service during that war. At that time, he was going to Hawaii, and some men were called back to Japan from Hawaii due to the war. This event influenced his movements and ultimately his immigration to America.

My husband went to Hawaii around the time of the Russo-Japanese War, and some men were called back to Japan from there because of the war. He was exempted from military service due to his injured hand. Hawaii was a stop in his journey before he came to America, and it was part of the story of how he eventually settled in the United States.

I always wanted to come to America, even though I didn't know much about it before I married my husband. We came to America in 1917 after our marriage in Japan. Life here was new and different, and my husband had already been in America before. My mother was reluctant to let me go, but since he was a relative, others thought it was okay. America became my new home where I helped my family and built a life.

After we got our visa and passport, we went to Yokohama to catch a ship to America. The ships were crowded with picture brides and other passengers, so we had to wait in Yokohama for about a month. We stayed at a hotel during that time, which was expensive, but it was better than going home and missing another ship. Yokohama was the last place in Japan we stayed before coming to America.

While we were waiting in Yokohama, we went to Tokyo to see my uncle who was a doctor doing research there. It was during this time that I learned many people from Okinawa were going to Brazil as government contract immigrants. Tokyo was a place where I connected with family and learned about other migration patterns while preparing to leave Japan.

During our preparations to leave Japan, I heard that many people from Okinawa were going to Brazil as government contract immigrants. This was something I learned while visiting Tokyo with my uncle. Although I was not from Okinawa, it was interesting to hear about their migration during that time as part of the broader movement of Japanese people abroad.

I heard that many people from Okinawa were going to Brazil as government contract immigrants when I was in Tokyo. We even booked passage on a ship going to South America because of this. Although I did not go to Brazil myself, it was part of the migration stories I encountered while preparing for my journey to America.

Although not directly mentioned in detail, San Francisco was likely part of the journey or destination when coming to America, as many Japanese immigrants arrived there. My husband had been in America before, and we came here in 1917. The immigration process involved passing health exams and waiting for ships, which were common experiences for those arriving through ports like San Francisco.

The transcript does not provide specific details about the Seiyo Maru, so I do not have personal memories or stories connected to that ship. However, ships like the Seiyo Maru were commonly used for transporting immigrants from Japan to America or other destinations during my time. Our journey involved waiting for ships in Yokohama, which were often crowded with picture brides and other passengers.

Angel Island is not directly mentioned in my story, but it was a common immigration station for Japanese arriving in America. After arriving by ship, many Japanese immigrants were processed there. My husband and I came to America in 1917, so it is possible that we passed through Angel Island during our immigration, as many did at that time.

Woodbridge, California, is not specifically mentioned in my story, but many Japanese immigrants settled in California farming communities like Woodbridge. My husband leased 10 acres of land when we came to America, and we worked hard to establish ourselves. California was the place where we built our new life after leaving Japan.

Lodi, California, is not directly referenced in my memories, but it was one of the areas where Japanese immigrants farmed and lived. Like many others, my husband and I worked the land after coming to America in 1917. Farming was a central part of our life and livelihood in California.

Asahi, California, is not mentioned directly in my story, but it was a community where many Japanese immigrants settled. After arriving in America, my husband leased farmland and we worked hard to support our family. Communities like Asahi were important centers for Japanese Americans during those early years.

I remember Empire's Day, which was celebrated on February 11th. On that day, we had to set up the hall for the ceremony at school. While the men did the work, a few girls, including myself, visited and talked with our teacher, who scolded us for being lazy and not helping. It was a memorable school event that showed the importance of hard work and discipline.
